state_description: Marine diesel oil (MDO) and heavy fuel oil (HFO) powered vessels covering Australian coastal and domestic navigation. National average energy intensity calibrated to AES 2025 Table F1 coastal bunkers (44.5 PJ / 30,000 million_tkm = 1,483 GJ/million_tkm). Fuel split: 65% MDO, 35% HFO reflects vessel type mix (smaller coastal vessels predominantly MDO; bulk carriers use HFO). Energy intensity declines to 1,320 GJ/million_tkm by 2050 via MARPOL Tier III engine improvements and fleet renewal.
cost_components_summary: Annualised non-fuel operating cost per million tonne-kilometres (real 2024 AUD), excluding explicit energy commodity purchases and carbon costs.
input_basis_notes: AES 2025 Table F1 coastal bunkers (2023-24): 44.5 PJ / 30,000 million_tkm = 1,483 GJ/million_tkm. Split: MDO 964 (65%), HFO 519 (35%) GJ/million_tkm. MDO 2050: 860, HFO 2050: 460 GJ/million_tkm (efficiency improvement).
emissions_boundary_notes: Direct scope 1 fuel combustion only. Electricity upstream (scope 2) excluded; captured by the electricity supply family. Ammonia combustion emits no CO2 (green ammonia assumed). No process emissions for shipping.
rollout_limit_notes: National adoption bound reflecting vessel retirement cycles (~25-30 yr life), port infrastructure availability, and coastal trade regulatory environment.
evidence_summary: AES 2025 Table F1 coastal bunkers; BITRE coastal freight statistics; NGGI domestic navigation.
derivation_method: Energy coefficients from AES 2025 Table F1 coastal bunkers (44.5 PJ domestic water transport, 2023-24 / 30,000 million_tkm = 1483 GJ/million_tkm). Fuel split: 65% MDO (964.0 GJ), 35% HFO (519.0 GJ). NGGI check: 112.7 × 30,000 / 1e6 = 3.38 MtCO2e.
review_notes: Coastal navigation is heterogeneous (bulk carriers, ferries, offshore supply). Phase 2: disaggregate by vessel type and route.
candidate_expansion_pathway: Disaggregate into bulk carriers, ro-ro ferries, and offshore supply vessels.
times_or_vedalang_mapping_notes: Maps to domestic navigation demand technology.

state_description: Battery-electric propulsion for short-range coastal vessels and ferries. Electric drivetrain efficiency advantage (~1.5× vs diesel for comparable service) partially offset by battery weight and charging overhead. Energy intensity 450 GJ/million_tkm improving to 380 GJ/million_tkm by 2050. Limited to short coastal routes and vehicle/passenger ferries. Zero scope 1 CO2e (electricity scope 2 excluded per A003).
cost_components_summary: Annualised non-fuel operating cost per million tonne-kilometres (real 2024 AUD), excluding explicit energy commodity purchases and carbon costs.
input_basis_notes: Battery-electric vessel: 450 GJ electricity/million_tkm (2025) improving to 380 GJ/million_tkm (2050) through improved battery energy density and power electronics. Scope 1 CO2e = 0; electricity scope 2 excluded per A003.
emissions_boundary_notes: Direct scope 1 fuel combustion only. Electricity upstream (scope 2) excluded; captured by the electricity supply family. Ammonia combustion emits no CO2 (green ammonia assumed). No process emissions for shipping.
rollout_limit_notes: National adoption bound reflecting vessel retirement cycles (~25-30 yr life), port infrastructure availability, and coastal trade regulatory environment.
evidence_summary: Norled Ampere ferry (Norway) performance data; BC Ferries electrification; ARENA ferry electrification review 2023; CSIRO GenCost battery costs.
derivation_method: Energy coefficients from AES 2025 Table F1 coastal bunkers (44.5 PJ domestic water transport, 2023-24 / 30,000 million_tkm = 1483 GJ/million_tkm). Fuel split: 65% MDO (964.0 GJ), 35% HFO (519.0 GJ). NGGI check: 112.7 × 30,000 / 1e6 = 3.38 MtCO2e.
review_notes: Battery range limits applicability to short routes (<100 km). Max share of 25% by 2050 reflects the fraction of domestic coastal task on short routes. Charging infrastructure investment required.
candidate_expansion_pathway: Add explicit route-length feasibility and charging infrastructure as constraints.
times_or_vedalang_mapping_notes: Maps to electric domestic shipping demand node.

state_description: Ammonia-fuelled vessels using green ammonia (produced from renewable hydrogen). Ammonia combustion produces no CO2 (only N2 and H2O if combustion is complete). Energy intensity higher than diesel due to lower energy density of ammonia and conversion losses (1,200 GJ/million_tkm → 1,000 GJ/million_tkm by 2050). Zero scope 1 CO2e if green ammonia supply assumed. Applicable to bulk coastal freight where longer routes preclude battery-electric.
cost_components_summary: Annualised non-fuel operating cost per million tonne-kilometres (real 2024 AUD), excluding explicit energy commodity purchases and carbon costs.
input_basis_notes: Ammonia vessel energy intensity: 1,200 GJ/million_tkm (2025) reflecting lower energy density and dual-fuel engine inefficiency, improving to 1,000 GJ/million_tkm by 2050. Scope 1 CO2e = 0 (green ammonia, no CO2 in combustion).
emissions_boundary_notes: Direct scope 1 fuel combustion only. Electricity upstream (scope 2) excluded; captured by the electricity supply family. Ammonia combustion emits no CO2 (green ammonia assumed). No process emissions for shipping.
rollout_limit_notes: National adoption bound reflecting vessel retirement cycles (~25-30 yr life), port infrastructure availability, and coastal trade regulatory environment.
evidence_summary: MAN Energy Solutions ammonia engine data; DNV ammonia-fuelled vessel study 2022; CSIRO ammonia fuel pathway analysis; IMO GHG decarbonisation strategy.
derivation_method: Energy coefficients from AES 2025 Table F1 coastal bunkers (44.5 PJ domestic water transport, 2023-24 / 30,000 million_tkm = 1483 GJ/million_tkm). Fuel split: 65% MDO (964.0 GJ), 35% HFO (519.0 GJ). NGGI check: 112.7 × 30,000 / 1e6 = 3.38 MtCO2e.
review_notes: Ammonia combustion NOx and N2O emissions require careful accounting (N2O has high GWP). Phase 2: add N2O combustion emission factors. Green ammonia supply chain immature. Max share 20% by 2050 is exploratory.
candidate_expansion_pathway: Add N2O emission factor for ammonia combustion; explicit green ammonia supply chain.
times_or_vedalang_mapping_notes: Maps to ammonia domestic shipping node; ammonia from dedicated supply family.

derivation_method: Energy coefficients from AES 2025 Table F1 coastal bunkers (44.5 PJ domestic water transport, 2023-24 / 30,000 million_tkm = 1483 GJ/million_tkm). Fuel split: 65% MDO (964.0 GJ), 35% HFO (519.0 GJ). NGGI check: 112.7 × 30,000 / 1e6 = 3.38 MtCO2e.
review_notes: Ammonia combustion NOx and N2O emissions require careful accounting (N2O has high GWP). Phase 2: add N2O combustion emission factors. Green ammonia supply chain immature. Max share 20% by 2050 is exploratory.
candidate_expansion_pathway: Add N2O emission factor for ammonia combustion; explicit green ammonia supply chain.
times_or_vedalang_mapping_notes: Maps to ammonia domestic shipping node; ammonia from dedicated supply family.

state_description: Ammonia-fuelled vessels using green ammonia (produced from renewable hydrogen). Ammonia combustion produces no CO2 (only N2 and H2O if combustion is complete). Energy intensity higher than diesel due to lower energy density of ammonia and conversion losses (1,200 GJ/million_tkm → 1,000 GJ/million_tkm by 2050). Zero scope 1 CO2e if green ammonia supply assumed. Applicable to bulk coastal freight where longer routes preclude battery-electric.
cost_components_summary: Annualised non-fuel operating cost per million tonne-kilometres (real 2024 AUD), excluding explicit energy commodity purchases and carbon costs.
input_basis_notes: Ammonia vessel energy intensity: 1,200 GJ/million_tkm (2025) reflecting lower energy density and dual-fuel engine inefficiency, improving to 1,000 GJ/million_tkm by 2050. Scope 1 CO2e = 0 (green ammonia, no CO2 in combustion).
emissions_boundary_notes: Direct scope 1 fuel combustion only. Electricity upstream (scope 2) excluded; captured by the electricity supply family. Ammonia combustion emits no CO2 (green ammonia assumed). No process emissions for shipping.
rollout_limit_notes: National adoption bound reflecting vessel retirement cycles (~25-30 yr life), port infrastructure availability, and coastal trade regulatory environment.
